hero.title: Glashapullagh
hero.subtitle: A working peatland restoration landscape in West Limerick. Explore the science, craft, and long-term stewardship of bogland recovery.

items.title: Restoration
items.description: Active rewetting and ecological engineering of degraded peatland to restore hydrological function and biodiversity.

items.title: Science
items.description: Rigorous monitoring of peat accumulation, water tables, vegetation dynamics, and carbon sequestration over time.

items.title: Stewardship
items.description: Long-term land management integrating traditional knowledge with contemporary ecological science.

items.title: Community
items.description: Engagement and education programs connecting local stakeholders to peatland conservation and restoration.

parallaxBreaks.title: Understanding Peatlands
parallaxBreaks.text: Peatlands cover only 3% of Earth's land surface, yet they store twice as much carbon as all forests combined. These fragile ecosystems are critical for climate regulation and biodiversity.

parallaxBreaks.title: The Challenge
parallaxBreaks.text: Centuries of drainage, peat extraction, and agricultural use have degraded 90% of Western European peatlands. Restoration requires patient, adaptive management and long-term commitment.

about.label: About
about.title: The Glashapullagh Project
paragraphs: Glashapullagh is a working peatland restoration landscape in West Limerick, Ireland. Spanning 287 hectares of formerly drained and degraded raised and blanket bog, the site represents a long-term commitment to ecological restoration and sustainable land stewardship.

Since 2009, we have conducted extensive hydrological rewetting, vegetation management, and scientific monitoring. Our approach combines traditional ecological knowledge with contemporary restoration science, demonstrating how degraded peatlands can be returned to ecological health and carbon functionality.

The project serves as both a living laboratory and a model for large-scale peatland restoration across the Atlantic Boglands region. We work collaboratively with researchers, policymakers, landowners, and local communities to advance understanding of bog restoration and climate mitigation.

topoMap.label: Location
topoMap.title: Topographical Overview
topoMap.description: Interactive map of the Glashapullagh restoration site in West Limerick

items.title: Rewetting
items.description: Strategic blocking of drainage channels and installation of weirs to raise water tables and restore hydrological connectivity.

items.title: Vegetation Management
items.description: Targeted removal of invasive species and encouragement of native Sphagnum mosses and ericaceous plants.

items.title: Habitat Creation
items.description: Installation of water bodies and microtopography to increase habitat diversity and support specialized peatland fauna.

items.title: Peat Accumulation
items.description: Annual measurement of new peat formation using marked horizons and soil cores.

items.title: Water Dynamics
items.description: Continuous monitoring of water tables, pH, and nutrient cycling across the site.

items.title: Vegetation Survey
items.description: Regular quadrat surveys tracking plant community composition and cover changes.

items.title: Fauna Monitoring
items.description: Bird surveys, invertebrate assessments, and rare species tracking programs.

items.title: Carbon Flux
items.description: Chamber measurements of CO₂ and methane emissions and sequestration rates.

items.title: Long-term Data
items.description: 15+ year dataset providing unprecedented insights into bog recovery timescales.

items.title: Introduction to Glashapullagh
items.description: An overview of the project, landscape, and restoration vision.

items.title: Rewetting the Bog
items.description: Documentary on the hydrological restoration process and water management.

items.title: Life Returns
items.description: Following the recovery of plant and animal communities over a decade.

items.title: Monitoring for Impact
items.description: Science in action: the research methods driving adaptive management.

items.title: Climate Benefits
items.description: How peatland restoration contributes to carbon sequestration and climate mitigation.

items.title: Community Engagement
items.description: Local partnerships and educational programs connecting people to the landscape.
